एतद्योनीनि भूतानि सर्वाणीत्युपधारय ।
अहं कृत्स्नस्य जगतः प्रभवः प्रलयस्तथा ॥ ६ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
etad-yonīni bhūtāni sarvāṇīty upadhāraya |
ahaṁ kṛtsnasya jagataḥ prabhavaḥ pralayas tathā || 6 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic edition; text body identical; punctuation/danda rendering differs).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_07.006 = MBh_06,029.006). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: having these (two prakṛtis) as their womb / source

translations.verbatim_quote: All created beings have their source in these two natures. Of all that is material and all that is spiritual in this world, know for certain that I am both the origin and the dissolution.
Know that all living beings are manifested by these two energies of Mine. I am the source of the entire creation, and into Me it again dissolves.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Everything that exists is a product of matter and spirit. Spirit is the basic field of creation, and matter is created by spirit. Spirit is not created at a certain stage of material development.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.6 Upadhāraya, understand; iti, thus; that sarvāṇi, all; bhūtāni, things; etat-yonīni, have these (etat) as their source (yoni) — things that have these lower and higher Prakṛtis, characterized as the 'field' and the 'Knower of the field', as their source.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara explicitly maps the two prakṛtis to the kṣetra/kṣetrajña distinction (Ch. 13's field/knower-of-the-field). All beings arise from these two, and Krishna is the origin and dissolution of the entire universe — beginning, middle, and end.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.6 Know that all beings from Brahma down to a tuft of grass, who have their origin in these two Prakṛtis of Mine, are aggregated forms of the self and of inanimate matter. Irrespective of whether they are existing in a superior or inferior form, the selves and matter are mixed.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja reads the two prakṛtis as the constituents of every actual being from Brahma to grass. Every being is an aggregate of self (parā) and inanimate matter (aparā). Krishna as origin/dissolution names his role as the source and end of the entire mixed universe.

positions.rendering: all created beings have their source in these two natures; I am origin and dissolution of all that is material and spiritual
all living beings are manifested by these two energies; the entire creation arises in Me and dissolves into Me
the two prakṛtis are kṣetra and kṣetrajña (Ch. 13); Krishna is origin/dissolution of the entire universe
every being from Brahma to grass is an aggregate of self and matter; Krishna is the source and end of the aggregate

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: The translators agree that Krishna names the two prakṛtis as the origin of all beings, and himself as the origin and dissolution of the universe. Shankara reads the two prakṛtis through the kṣetra/kṣetrajña frame that the Gita develops fully in Chapter 13 — a forward reference. Ramanuja's emphasis is on the aggregated character of every actual being: no jīva exists except in some material form, and no material form is animated except by some jīva. The two readings are compatible at the surface but diverge on the metaphysics.

literal_meaning: Understand that all beings have these two as their womb. I am the origin of the entire universe, and likewise the dissolution.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna draws the implication of 7.4-7.5: since all beings arise from the joining of his lower and higher prakṛtis, he himself is the origin and dissolution (prabhavaḥ pralayaḥ) of the entire universe. The verse compresses the Sāṅkhya cosmogony (matter + soul → embodied beings) and assigns its source to Krishna. The chapter's metaphysical reach begins to extend here; 7.7 will give the famous beads-on-thread image as the structural illustration.
